properties.name: Athabasca Oil Sands
properties.productionContext: ~3.0+ million bbl/d of bitumen — the single largest producing region in Canada
properties.reserves: ~165 billion bbl recoverable bitumen (third-largest oil reserves on Earth, after Venezuela and Saudi Arabia)
properties.geology: Cretaceous-age McMurray Formation sands saturated with bitumen. Depths range from surface outcrop (mineable) to ~500m (in-situ).
keyFacts: Produces over half of Canada's total crude oil output.
Bitumen is too thick to flow naturally — recovered via either open-pit mining (~20% of production) or SAGD / steam-assisted gravity drainage (~80%).
Fort McMurray is the operational hub; the city's population swings ~10–20% with construction-phase activity cycles.
Carbon intensity per barrel is higher than most conventional crude — central tension in Canadian energy/climate policy.
Pathways Alliance (Suncor + CNRL + Cenovus + others) is proposing a multi-billion-dollar CCUS network; FID still pending as of 2026.
properties.notes: Polygon is a simplified outline of the producing region around Fort McMurray. Actual deposit boundaries are larger and irregular.

properties.name: Cold Lake Oil Sands
properties.reserves: ~200 billion bbl bitumen in place (recoverable subset is smaller)
properties.geology: Cretaceous Clearwater Formation; deposits sit deeper than Athabasca — economically recoverable only via in-situ thermal methods (CSS — cyclic steam stimulation, and SAGD).
keyFacts: The second-largest oil sands deposit by production — but with deposits too deep to mine, all production is in-situ.
Imperial Oil's Cold Lake project is the country's largest single thermal in-situ operation.
CSS (cyclic steam stimulation) was pioneered here — inject steam, soak, then produce on huff-and-puff cycles.
Lower carbon intensity than mining but higher than Montney / Duvernay gas/liquids production.
Connected to the Enbridge Mainline + Trans Mountain via gathering systems through Edmonton.

properties.name: Peace River Oil Sands
properties.geology: Cretaceous Bluesky-Gething Formation, deeper than Athabasca and lower-permeability than Cold Lake.
keyFacts: The smallest and northwestern-most of Canada's three oil sands deposits.
Lower production rate than Athabasca and Cold Lake — geology is less favourable, with less continuous reservoir.
All current production is in-situ (SAGD) — no surface mining viable here.
Often gets less media attention but completes the 'three oil sands deposits' story (Athabasca + Cold Lake + Peace River).
Pipeline access via TransCanada Northwest connector → Edmonton hub.

properties.name: Montney
properties.primaryResource: Natural gas + NGLs / condensate (the marquee gas + liquids play)
properties.productionContext: ~12 Bcf/d of gas + ~700 thousand bbl/d of condensate and NGLs (Canada's most prolific producer)
properties.reserves: ~500+ Tcf marketable gas resource (resource estimates have grown materially over the past decade as drilling tested deeper / wider)
properties.geology: Triassic Montney Formation — siltstone reservoir with two distinct pay zones (Upper and Lower). Spans NE BC across the AB border.
keyFacts: Canada's most prolific gas play — supplies Coastal GasLink (LNG Canada) and most prairie-export gas pipelines.
Liquids-rich (condensate + NGLs) — operators chase the 'liquids window' for higher netbacks vs dry-gas-only zones.
The condensate stream is critical: it's blended with bitumen to produce 'dilbit' (diluted bitumen) for pipeline transport from the oil sands.
Montney rivals US shale plays for resource size — debated as one of the largest natural gas reservoirs globally.
Tourmaline Oil is the largest single producer (relevant for the upcoming Tourmaline DCF on this dashboard).
properties.notes: Polygon spans the BC / AB border around Dawson Creek, Fort St. John, and Grande Prairie. The Montney is geologically more extensive than the rendered outline.

properties.name: Duvernay
properties.primaryResource: Light oil + condensate + natural gas (very NGL-rich)
properties.productionContext: ~250–300 thousand boe/d, weighted toward liquids and condensate
properties.reserves: ~100+ Tcf gas resource and billions of bbl of recoverable liquids
properties.geology: Upper Devonian Duvernay Formation — organic-rich shale that sources hydrocarbons trapped in adjacent rock. Recovered via horizontal drilling + multi-stage hydraulic fracturing.
keyFacts: Canada's premier liquids-rich shale — biggest source of domestic condensate, which is essential for pipeline-shipping bitumen as 'dilbit'.
Operators target the 'volatile oil window' where liquids yields are highest.
Located beneath the Deep Basin — both plays often produced from the same well pads.
Younger commercially than the Montney — most production has come online in the past decade.
Often described as Alberta's analogue to the US Eagle Ford (south Texas) or Bakken — but with deeper carbonates above and below.
properties.notes: Polygon is centred over the Drayton Valley / Edson / Fox Creek corridor. The Duvernay is geologically more extensive than the rendered outline, and underlies parts of the Montney territory at greater depth.

properties.name: Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin
properties.primaryResource: Geological umbrella for Canadian oil sands, gas, and NGL plays
properties.productionContext: WCSB-wide ~5+ million boe/d combined. The five named basins on this map are sub-regions within it.
properties.reserves: ~165 billion bbl bitumen reserves + 500+ Tcf gas resource — Canada's energy heartland
majorOperators: See individual basins for the operator-level breakdown
properties.geology: Foreland basin formed when the Rocky Mountains thrust upward and the adjacent crust flexed downward, creating an asymmetric trough deepest along the western edge. Sediments stratify from Cambrian–Devonian carbonates at depth to Cretaceous shales and sandstones near surface — every formation is a potential producer.
keyFacts: Spans most of Alberta and Saskatchewan, with extensions into NE British Columbia, southwestern Manitoba, and the southern Northwest Territories.
Hosts ~95% of Canada's onshore oil and natural gas production.
All five named basins on this map (Athabasca, Cold Lake, Peace River, Montney, Duvernay) are specific producing zones inside the WCSB.

Cretaceous shales (Montney, Duvernay) source most of the gas + liquids; Cretaceous sandstones (McMurray Formation, etc.) host the oil sands; the Devonian carbonates beneath were the original conventional oil targets.
Most analyst conversations about Canadian oil & gas reference 'WCSB' as shorthand for the Western Canadian production base.
properties.notes: Hand-traced approximation. The actual basin runs from the Rocky Mountain Foreland (west) to the Canadian Shield edge (east), and from the US border (south) to roughly 60°N (north). Renders as a dashed backdrop behind the production basins to communicate its 'canvas' role.
